title: Big funds are super heartless
full_text:
The biggest revelation in the report on failing superannuation standards is not a technology bug or global impacts. It’s that the care factor failure is systemic.
The report by Super Consumers Australia revealed Australian Super did not bother to answer 90 per cent of calls and that 70 per cent of calls from customers experiencing vulnerability scored five out of 10 or less for empathy.
Super belongs to people for their retirement. The same goes for death benefits. There are over 4000 people waiting up to four years to access their own money. One of the first things I did when assisting constituents on late death benefits was to check what the mandated minimum payout time frame was with the regulator, ASIC. Surely there must be one.
It turns out there is not. ASIC just says it should be “as soon as practicable”. In other words, there is no mandated minimum time frame. There needs to be.
In January 2025, the federal government announced plans to introduce mandatory service standards for super funds.
Big super has made an art form of frustrating every effort at every turn. Transferring calls, not answering emails, and challenging every document.
In many cases we are talking about older women, who while navigating the grief of losing their spouse have to jump through every obstacle imaginable to get their money.
A light-handed, industry-led code of conduct was implemented a couple of years ago, around the time complaints to the ombudsman tripled. It’s not working.
The ASIC Claims Handling Review has released a report revealing that 78 per cent of reviewed death benefit claim files were delayed by processing issues within the trustees’ control.
While some cases have complexities that need to be worked through, this shows that nearly 80 per cent of delayed cases are in fact straight forward.
It’s time big super was held to account. It’s well past time for this government to act. Mary Aldred is the Liberal member for Monash
